notes: v2 is the subtle set: where v1 saturated on detection (every model finds an obvious removed check), these bugs need data-flow and boolean tracing — a missing JWT claim, a one-character operator flip, a removed defensive line, an injection in ORDER BY that people assume is safe. Detection itself should separate models here, not just severity.

bugs.description: The password-reset token no longer carries an `exp` claim — the `expires`/`exp` values are still computed but dropped from the JWT payload (`{"nbf": now, "sub": email}`). The token therefore never expires: a reset link captured from an email, a proxy log, or browser history stays valid forever. The tell is subtle — the code still looks like it handles expiry (the variables are right there), it just stops putting it in the token.

injection.find:
encoded_jwt = jwt.encode(
        {"exp": exp, "nbf": now, "sub": email},
        settings.SECRET_KEY,
        algorithm=security.ALGORITHM,
    )
injection.replace_with:
encoded_jwt = jwt.encode(
        {"nbf": now, "sub": email},
        settings.SECRET_KEY,
        algorithm=security.ALGORITHM,
    )

bugs.description: The ownership guard in update_item has its comparison flipped from `!=` to `==`: `if not current_user.is_superuser and (item.owner_id == current_user.id): raise 403`. The effect is inverted — a non-superuser is now blocked from editing items they OWN, and permitted to edit items they do NOT own. A one-character change that turns the authorization check inside out; you only catch it by tracing the boolean for both the owner and non-owner cases.

The JWT decode allowlist now includes `"none"`: `algorithms=[security.ALGORITHM, "none"]`. By accepting the `none` algorithm, the server will honor a token whose header declares `alg: none` and which carries no signature at all — classic JWT algorithm-confusion. An attacker forges an unsigned token with any `sub` and is authenticated as that user. Subtle because the legitimate algorithm is still listed right beside it.

The constant-time defense in `authenticate` was removed. Previously, when the email didn't exist the code still ran `verify_password(password, DUMMY_HASH)` so the response time matched the user-exists case; now it returns immediately on a missing user. That early return reintroduces a timing side channel: a fast 'incorrect password' means the email isn't registered, a slow one means it is — a user-enumeration oracle. The hardest bug in the set: nothing looks wrong, a defensive line is simply gone.

injection.find:
db_user = get_user_by_email(session=session, email=email)
    if not db_user:
        # Prevent timing attacks by running password verification even when user doesn't exist
        # This ensures the response time is similar whether or not the email exists
        verify_password(password, DUMMY_HASH)
        return None

bugs.description: Password recovery used to always return the same generic message to avoid revealing whether an email is registered. It now raises a 404 'user does not exist' when the email isn't found, so an attacker can enumerate valid accounts through the public, unauthenticated recovery endpoint. The send-email logic still works; the leak is purely in the branch that now distinguishes the two cases.

read_users gained a `sort` query parameter that is interpolated straight into an `order_by(text(f"{sort} desc"))`. Even though it's 'only' an ORDER BY clause — which developers routinely assume is safe — it is raw SQL built from user input, so it is SQL injection (ORDER BY injection enables boolean/subquery exfiltration). Subtle because the dangerous part hides in a sort option, not in a WHERE filter.

injection.find:
def read_users(session: SessionDep, skip: int = 0, limit: int = 100) -> Any:
    """
    Retrieve users.
    """

    count_statement = select(func.count()).select_from(User)
    count = session.exec(count_statement).one()

    statement = (
        select(User).order_by(col(User.created_at).desc()).offset(skip).limit(limit)
    )
injection.replace_with:
def read_users(session: SessionDep, skip: int = 0, limit: int = 100, sort: str = "created_at") -> Any:
    """
    Retrieve users.
    """
    from sqlalchemy import text

    count_statement = select(func.count()).select_from(User)
    count = session.exec(count_statement).one()

    statement = (
        select(User).order_by(text(f"{sort} desc")).offset(skip).limit(limit)
    )
